content.problemStatement:
In C++, you can create user-defined exceptions by defining your own exception classes.  Creating your own exception classes allows you to provide more context-specific information about the error and customize how it is handled.

Here's how you can create user-defined exceptions in C++:
- ***Define a Custom Exception Class:*** To create a user-defined exception, you need to define a new class that is derived from the exception class or one of its derived classes. 
  - In this example, we've created a custom exception class MyCustomException that inherits from exception and provides a custom error message through the what() method.

- ***Throwing a User-Defined Exception:*** To throw an instance of your custom exception, you can use the throw keyword followed by an object of your exception class:
  - In this example, we throw an instance of MyCustomException with a custom error message, and then we catch and handle it in the catch block.
  - The what() method is overridden within MyCustomException. The what() method is a standard method in the std::exception class, and it returns a const char* representing an error message. In this case, it returns the msg member variable converted to a C-style string (const char*).

- ***Handling User-Defined Exceptions:*** When you catch a user-defined exception, you can handle it just like any other exception by providing code within the catch block to handle the error. You can access the error message or any other information provided by your custom exception class.
  - Within the try block, there is a throw statement that explicitly throws an instance of MyCustomException with the error message "Custom exception message."
  - In the catch block, the code catches the MyCustomException exception by reference (const MyCustomException& ex) and handles it.
  - The what() method of the caught exception (ex) is called to retrieve the error message, and it is printed to the standard output using cout.

When you run this program, it will throw the MyCustomException with the specified message and catch it within the catch block, printing the custom error message to the console.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
